petitioner_background: The petitioner is a physician from Taiwan (R.O.C.) specializing in research and clinical studies in Endodontics. He sought classification as an employment-based immigrant under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act as an alien of extraordinary ability in the sciences. He has worked at veterans hospitals in Taiwan, including the Taichung Veterans General Hospital, and has conducted research and published papers in dental journals. The petition was filed on June 25, 2003.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of: (1) 'The Best Doctor of 1991' award for 'Excellence in Clinical Performance' from the Veterans Affairs Commission, Executive R.O.C.; (2) 'Certificate of Achievement' from the 'ACADEMY OF ENDODNOTOLOGY [sic] R.O.C.' for completing 'an active member application' and demonstrating 'proficiency in the theory and practice of Endodontics' (1994); (3) Citation from the Taiwan Dental Association for ten years of distinguished social service (1994); (4) 'The Best Doctor of Year 1996' award from the Taichung City Health Bureau; (5) Letter of Thanks from the Tainan Dental Association (1998); (6) 'Service medal of 3rd grade' for achievement and ten years of continuous service at the Taichung Veterans General Hospital (2000); (7) 'The Best Doctor of Year 2001' award for 'Excellence in Clinical Instruction' from the Veterans Affairs Commission, Executive R.O.C. The petitioner also submitted a March 4, 2003 letter from the Veterans Affairs Commission describing how Best Doctor of the Year nominees are generated through departmental commendations and evaluated by an electing committee of ten members from each hospital's Executive Committee of Medical Education.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that items 1 and 7 (Best Doctor awards from the Veterans Affairs Commission) reflect departmental or institutional recognition rather than national recognition, based on the description of the selection process which occurs within each hospital. Item 2 (Certificate of Achievement) lacked evidence showing it was a nationally recognized award for excellence rather than simply an acknowledgment of completing basic certification requirements or training. Item 3 (Taiwan Dental Association citation) was not shown to constitute a top honor for excellence in his field, but rather an acknowledgment of ten years of distinguished social service, and the petitioner provided no evidence it was nationally recognized. Items 4, 5, and 6 were found to be local, regional, or institutional in scope rather than national. Overall, the petitioner failed to establish that his awards enjoyed significant national or international stature or were widely recognized beyond the organizations that presented them.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his membership in the Association for Dental Sciences, Taichung Dental Association, Academy of Endodontology, American Association of Endodontists, and the Asia-Pacific Endodontic Confederation.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the record did not include the membership bylaws or official admission requirements for these organizations. There was no indication that admission to membership required outstanding achievement or that the petitioner was evaluated by national or international experts in consideration of his admission. The AAO emphasized that membership requirements based on employment, activity in a field, minimum education or experience, test scores, GPA, recommendations, or dues payment do not satisfy this criterion, and that members must be selected at the national or international level rather than local or regional. The overall prestige of an association is not determinative; the issue is membership requirements.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of three Taiwanese newspaper articles, one of which appeared in the Liberty Times. None of the articles were accompanied by proper English language translations.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that pursuant to 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), foreign language documents submitted to CIS must be accompanied by a full English language translation certified as complete and accurate by a competent translator. The three articles did not meet these requirements. Without complete translations, the AAO could not conclude that the petitioner was the primary subject of the material or that he was featured because of his significant achievements. Additionally, the petitioner did not submit quantitative data regarding the volume of distribution of the newspapers, so the AAO could not conclude they qualified as 'major media' with substantial national readership. Finally, the evidence did not identify 'the title, date, and author of the material' as required by this criterion. The AAO also noted that to qualify as major media, a publication should have significant national or international distribution, and that an alien would not earn national or international acclaim from a local publication or from a publication in a language most of the population cannot comprehend.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his research projects, including six research reports that he prepared from 1991 through 1996. He also submitted two letters of support from professors who chair the dentistry departments at veterans hospitals where he has worked, indicating that the petitioner has performed admirably throughout his career and published his research.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that the record contained no evidence showing that any of the petitioner's research findings are viewed throughout his field as a contribution of major significance. The supporting letters offered no information regarding how the petitioner's work has significantly impacted the dentistry or endodontics fields. While authorship of published papers may demonstrate research yielded useful and valid results, the AAO noted that any article accepted for publication must offer new and useful information, but it does not follow that every individual whose research is published has made a major contribution. The AAO also noted that the source of recommendation letters from the petitioner's affiliated hospitals was a highly relevant consideration — these letters were not first-hand evidence of acclaim outside his affiliated institutions. An individual with sustained national or international acclaim should be able to provide ample unsolicited materials reflecting that acclaim. Without extensive documentation showing the petitioner's work has been unusually influential or highly acclaimed throughout the greater field, the AAO could not conclude his work rises to the level of a contribution of major significance.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence of his authorship of papers published in journals such as Clinical Dentistry and Journal of Dental Science.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that publication of scholarly papers is not presumptive evidence of sustained national or international acclaim; the greater field's reaction to those papers must also be considered. The very act of publication is not as reliable a gauge as the citation history of the published works. If a published article attracts the attention of other researchers, they will cite the source article, and numerous independent citations would provide firm evidence that the petitioner's work influenced others. Conversely, few or no citations would suggest the work has gone largely unnoticed. In this case, there was no evidence showing that the petitioner's published papers are widely cited or acclaimed outside his circle of acquaintances.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: On appeal, counsel argued that the petitioner's conference presentations satisfy this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found that this criterion is more appropriate for the visual arts (such as sculpting and painting) rather than scientific or medical research. In the fields of science and medicine, acclaim is generally not established by the mere act of presenting one's work at a conference. The AAO also found the record contained no documentation demonstrating that presenting one's work is unusual in the petitioner's field or that the invitation to present was a privilege extended to only a few top researchers. Participation in scientific conferences and symposia is routine and expected in the medical research community. The record contained no evidence showing that the petitioner's conference presentations commanded an unusual level of attention compared to other participants or that he served as a keynote speaker at a national or international scientific conference.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted evidence showing that from January 1, 2001, to December 31, 2001, his gross earnings were $4,415,287 TWD (Taiwan New Dollars). He also submitted average monthly salary statistics for 'Medical & Health Services' professionals in Taiwan.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner's reliance on 'average' salary statistics for 'Medical & Health Services' professionals was flawed for two reasons. First, the petitioner must submit evidence showing his salary is significantly high in relation to that of other endodontists (his specific field), but the evidence included no listing of the specific occupations under the broad category of 'Medical & Health Services' professionals. Second, the evidence must demonstrate his salary places him at the very top of his field rather than above average in his field.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that the petitioner failed to demonstrate he met at least three of the ten regulatory criteria required to establish sustained national or international acclaim. The review of the record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished himself to such an extent that he achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field. The evidence was not persuasive that the petitioner's achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. Therefore, the petitioner did not establish eligibility pursuant to section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act, and the burden of proof under Section 291 of the Act remained unsatisfied.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act
fraud_or_procedural_issues: The petitioner submitted three Taiwanese newspaper articles without proper English language translations, failing to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), which requires foreign language documents to be accompanied by a full certified English translation. The submitted evidence also failed to identify the title, date, and author of the published materials as required by the regulation.
